title: Exposure to emerging technologies like GenAI The initial role provided some work-from-home flexibility.
pros:
Exposure to emerging technologies like GenAI 
The initial role provided some work-from-home flexibility.
cons:
The negatives significantly outweigh any minor upsides and point to systemic issues, particularly within the internal AIA project.

No exposure to correct software engineering practices or Docker usage, which can be useful for you in your future career.

Joining & Compensation Issues: The joining process was severely delayed. Promotions and yearly increments are consistently delayed, often pushed back until August or even November, impacting overall compensation and morale.

Toxic Leadership & Culture (AIA): The project director cultivates a hostile and singular-vision environment. Leadership is a "one-man show" where the director's ideas are favored, regardless of team expertise. Employees face intense pressure, are shamed for taking necessary leaves, and are constantly pushed to compete and work excessive, late hours without clear benefit.

Ineffective Project Management: Employees are subjected to months of intense, late-night coding efforts only to have project ideas or prototypes dropped or ignored due to the director's sudden lack of confidence. The focus is on rushed, high-pressure development over quality, even when better technical knowledge is available on the team.

Arbitrary Performance Process: Performance rating is disconnected from actual effort. Ratings feel arbitrary, heavily dependent on the mood and favor of leadership, rather than objective performance metrics. As stated by the director, low-rating distributions mean hardworking individuals are consistently penalized.

Exploitative Exit Process: Even after resigning, the company attempted to prolong the notice period unnecessarily. Despite verbal promises and having no remaining dependency, the director intentionally delayed the Knowledge Transfer (KT) process and resource allocation for weeks, breaking a commitment to an early release and forcing the employee to beg for relief.

title: 1. Many consultants report a relatively good work-life balance compared to other IT service firms, depending on the project and client. 2. Cognizant offers structured career progression with clear roles and responsibilities, especially for consultants moving into lead or architect roles. (it depends if you have good network) 3. Access to internal training platforms, certifications, and SAP Learning Hub subscriptions to stay updated with the latest SAP technologies.
pros:
1. Many consultants report a relatively good work-life balance compared to other IT service firms, depending on the project and client.
2. Cognizant offers structured career progression with clear roles and responsibilities, especially for consultants moving into lead or architect roles. (it depends if you have good network)
3. Access to internal training platforms, certifications, and SAP Learning Hub subscriptions to stay updated with the latest SAP technologies.
cons:
1. Project Allocation Delays - Bench time can be unpredictable, especially between projects or during transitions between clients.
2. Compensation Structure - Salary increments and bonuses may not always match market expectations, especially for niche SAP skills.(they normalize the appraisal to often).
3. Limited Onsite Opportunities - Compared to some competitors, onsite roles (especially in Europe or the Middle East) may be fewer or harder to secure.(they just run some useless surveys to show we are taking interests from employees but no result).
4. Client-Driven Pressure - As with most service based companies, project timelines and deliverables are often dictated by clients, which can lead to high pressure situations.

They have a clear, well-trodden career ladder (e.g., Analyst → Associate → Senior Associate → Team Lead → Manager). You know exactly what is required to get to the next level in terms of years of experience and skills. In summary, service-based companies are not for everyone, but they are far from a sign of "lost hope."

They hire in very large numbers and are often more willing to take a chance on freshers with diverse educational backgrounds or lower GPAs compared to top product-based firms. cons:
The company underpays and then fails to provide annual increases that keep up with inflation so you will just get poorer and poorer with every passing year (last year if you were a top performer you got a 1.5% increase as an example.  This year if you were a Manager+ you got NO increase).  

The Indian culture is very much "do as I say".  

With a heavy presence in India you are working odd hours to talk to your Indian teammates who are wonderful to work with but theres a big time zone gap so you have to work earlier and later than normal, but the company is trying to force you to go to the office but when will you get there is the big question because you are working odd hours.
  
The company keeps saying the strategy is to "grow" which tells you everything you need to know about the company leadership; very few people even know what the strategy is.  

Despite all of this, the company asks for more from employees but they wont get more, people are very unhappy and very unwilling to work hard given theres zero incentive to do so from a compensation perspective.

title: On paper the role looks exciting and fulfilling. Health insurance is good. Can take career in a lot of directions given the size of the organization. Surprising number of talented people stick around in NA and EMEA. A good place to work if you are based in India. Can hide if you find a pocket.
pros: On paper the role looks exciting and fulfilling. Health insurance is good. Can take career in a lot of directions given the size of the organization. Surprising number of talented people stick around in NA and EMEA. A good place to work if you are based in India. Can hide if you find a pocket.
cons: Way behind the market on most criteria. India-based benchmarks mean the bar for what quality looks like is very low and the business today will likely be replaced by AI in a few years. You can feel the pressure/stress in the business as they cut all investments and delay or eliminate raises (especially for senior folks who they hope will take the hint and leave). Employee experience is transactional and treating people as commodities no matter how unique their skill set is. The culture is full of competitive “leaders” (use that word to define their position not the skill set) who steal work and credit from each other instead of working together. Works on a barter / favors system and you have to be “in the club” to get ahead (have heard it called the India mafia). Policies are overly complex and black hole operations are debilitating when you need to get stuff done. No way to get exceptions so if you are delivering outside of tech implementation work, you won’t be successful. Layers and layers of approvals - zero trust in leaders to make contextual decisions. Constant layoffs as they move everyone off shore to India so they can increase margins and give clients lower skilled talent for the same money. Have lost many discrimination lawsuits about this as the #1 H1B Visa provider in the US.  Becoming lower-tiered Infosys with the influx of leaders from that company since Ravi joined, most of whom are over their heads and unable to grasp the complexity of the business but fit the low-bar ethical and ethnicity mold. Used to talk about DEI but have failed in attracting or retaining women - most now being exited and numbers dropping quickly.

title: - Diverse Technical Exposure: Hands-on experience with Java, Spring Boot, microservices, and scalable backend systems across varied domains. - Client Interaction: Opportunities to work directly with clients, enhancing communication and stakeholder management skills. - Learning Resources: Access to internal platforms like Cognizant Academy and Udemy for continuous learning. - Team Culture: Supportive peers and collaborative environment in most accounts. - Stability: Job security and structured processes make it a reliable place to grow early in your career.
pros:
- Diverse Technical Exposure: Hands-on experience with Java, Spring Boot, microservices, and scalable backend systems across varied domains.
- Client Interaction: Opportunities to work directly with clients, enhancing communication and stakeholder management skills.
- Learning Resources: Access to internal platforms like Cognizant Academy and Udemy for continuous learning.
- Team Culture: Supportive peers and collaborative environment in most accounts.
- Stability: Job security and structured processes make it a reliable place to grow early in your career.
cons:
- Work-Life Balance: Highly dependent on the project; some accounts demand extended hours, 24/7 support and weekend work.
- Limited Tech Ownership: Decisions often driven by client preferences, leaving little room for innovation.
- Slow Career Progression: Appraisal cycles and promotions can feel sluggish despite consistent performance.
- Internal Mobility: Switching projects or roles internally involves bureaucratic hurdles.
